Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ عَدُوِّى وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِمَا جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْحَقِّ يُخْرِجُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۙ أَن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ رَبِّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهَـٰدًا فِى سَبِيلِى وَٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِى ۚ تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ وَأَنَا۠ أَعْلَمُ بِمَآ أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَمَآ أَعْلَنتُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْهُ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (١)
O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, [secretly] offering them affection, if you have set out for jihad in My way and to seek My pleasure, for they have certainly denied whatever has come to you of the truth, expelling the Apostle and you, because you have faith in Allah, your Lord. You secretly nourish affection for them, while I know well whatever you hide and whatever you disclose, and whoever among you does that has certainly strayed from the right way. (1)
إِن يَثْقَفُوكُمْ يَكُونُوا۟ لَكُمْ أَعْدَآءً وَيَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ وَأَلْسِنَتَهُم بِٱلسُّوٓءِ وَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٢)
If they were to confront you they would be your enemies, and would stretch out against you their hands and [unleash] their tongues with evil [intentions], and they are eager that you [too] should be faithless. (2)
لَن تَنفَعَكُمْ أَرْحَامُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ ۚ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٣)
Your relatives and children will not avail you on the Day of Resurrection: He will separate you [from one another], and Allah watches what you do. (3)

Content of Surah Al-Mumtahanah

This Surah is essentially composed of two sections:

The first section: It speaks of the concept of "Love for the sake of Allah" and "Hate for the sake of Allah," and the prohibition of befriending the polytheists. It invites Muslims to draw inspiration from the great Prophet of God, Ibrahim عليه السلام, and enumerates other characteristics in this regard.

This theme, as presented at the beginning of the Surah, is repeated and emphasized at its conclusion.

The second section: It discusses the migrant women, their testing and examination, and other rulings related to this matter. The choice of the name "Al-Mumtahanah" (The Woman to be Examined) for this Surah is due to this very issue of examination mentioned in verse 10. [^1] Another name mentioned for the Surah is "Surah al-Mawaddah" (The Surah of Affection) because of the prohibition of affection toward the polytheists in the first verse of the Surah.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah Al-Mumtahanah

In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"من قرء سورة الممتحنة كان المؤمنون و المؤمنات له شفعاء يوم القيامة"

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Mumtahanah, all believing men and believing women will be his intercessors on the Day of Resurrection." [^1] In another Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, it is stated:

"من قرء سورة الممتحنة فى فرائضه و نوافله امتحن اللَّه قلبه للايمان، و نور له بصره، و لا يصيبه فقر ابدا، و لا جنون فى بدنه، و لا فى ولده"

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Mumtahanah in his obligatory and supererogatory prayers, Allah will purify and prepare his heart for faith, grant him the light of insight, and poverty shall never afflict him, nor shall he or his children be afflicted with insanity." [^2] It goes without saying that all these virtues and honors belong to those who pay heed to the verses of this Surah regarding "Love for the sake of Allah," "Hate for the sake of Allah," and "Jihad in the way of Allah," and implement its content, rather than sufficing with a soulless recitation devoid of knowledge and action.

[^1]: Some have recited it as "Al-Mumtahanah" (with a fatha on the ha), referring to the migrant women who were being examined, and some have recited it as "Al-Mumtahina" (with a kasra on the ha) because the Surah itself is the means of examination.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 267.

[^2]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 299.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَ عَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَ قَدْ كَفَرُوا بِما جاءَكُمْ مِنَ الْحَقِّ يُخْرِجُونَ الرَّسُولَ وَ إِيَّاكُمْ أَنْ تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ رَبِّكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهاداً فِي سَبِيلِي وَ ابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِي تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَ أَنَا أَعْلَمُ بِما أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَ ما أَعْلَنْتُمْ وَ مَنْ يَفْعَلْهُ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ (1) إِنْ يَثْقَفُوكُمْ يَكُونُوا لَكُمْ أَعْداءً وَ يَبْسُطُوا إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ وَ أَلْسِنَتَهُمْ بِالسُّوءِ وَ وَدُّوا لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ (2) لَنْ تَنْفَعَكُمْ أَرْحامُكُمْ وَ لا أَوْلادُكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (3)

Occasion of Revelation

Most commentators have explicitly stated that these verses (or the first verse) were revealed concerning "Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah" (albeit with slight variations). We mention below what the late Tabarsi recorded in Majma' al-Bayan: The incident was that a woman named "Sarah," who was affiliated with one of the tribes of Mecca, came from Mecca to Medina to see the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked her: "Have you become a Muslim and come here?" She replied, "No." He asked: "Have you come as a migrant (Muhajirah)?"

She said: "No."

He asked: "Then why have you come?" She replied: "You were our kin and clan, and my providers have all gone; I have become severely needy and have come to you so that you may give me something and grant me clothes and a mount."

He said: "Then what happened to the youths of Mecca?" (Referring to the fact that the woman was a singer and used to sing for the youths).

She said: "After the event of Badr, no one requested me to sing," (and this shows how heavy the blow of the Battle of Badr was upon the polytheists of Mecca).

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the sons of Abd al-Muttalib to give her clothes, a mount, and travel expenses, and this was while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was preparing for the Conquest of Mecca.

At this time, "Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah" (one of the well-known Muslims who had participated in the Battle of Badr and the Pledge of Ridwan) came to Sarah, wrote a letter, and said: "Give this to the people of Mecca," and he gave her ten dinars—and according to some, ten dirhams—and also gifted her a cloak.

Hatib had written in the letter to the people of Mecca: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله intends to come toward you; be prepared to defend yourselves!" Sarah took the letter and set out from Medina toward Mecca.

Gabriel informed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of this matter. The Messenger of Allah ordered Ali عليه السلام, Ammar, Umar, Zubayr, Talhah, Miqdad, and Abu Marthad to mount their horses and head toward Mecca. He said: "At one of the stations along the way, you will reach a woman carrying a letter from Hatib to the polytheists of Mecca; take the letter from her."

They set out and reached her at the very place the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله had described. She swore that she had no letter with her. They searched her belongings and found nothing. They all decided to return, but Ali عليه السلام said: "Neither the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has lied to us, nor do we lie." He drew his sword and said: "Produce the letter, or by Allah, I will strike your neck!" When Sarah saw the matter was serious, she brought out the letter which she had hidden within her braids. They brought the letter to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent for Hatib and asked: "Do you recognize this letter?!" He replied: "Yes." He asked: "What caused you to do this?!" He said: "O Messenger of Allah! By Allah, from the day I accepted Islam, I have not been a disbeliever for a moment, I have never betrayed you, and I have never responded to the call of the polytheists since the time I separated from them. But the issue is that all the Migrants (Muhajirun) have people in Mecca who protect their families against the polytheists, but I am a stranger among them and my family is trapped in their clutches. I wanted to have a favor upon them through this so they would not bother my family, while I knew that Allah would ultimately grant them defeat, and my letter would be of no benefit to them."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accepted his excuse, but Umar stood up and said: "O Messenger of Allah! Let me strike the neck of this hypocrite!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "He is among the warriors of Badr, and Allah has a special look of grace upon them." (It was here that the above verses were revealed and gave important lessons regarding the abandonment of any friendship toward the polytheists and enemies of God to the Muslims). [^1]

Commentary

The Consequence of Befriending the Enemy of God

As we learned from the occasion of revelation, an action was taken by one of the Muslims which, although not intended as espionage, was considered an expression of affection toward the enemies of Islam. Therefore, the above verses were revealed to warn the Muslims to avoid repeating such actions in the future.

First, He says: "O you who have believed! Do not take My enemy and your enemy as your allies" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَ عَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ). This means they are not only the enemies of Allah but also harbor enmity and hostility toward you; despite this, how can you extend the hand of friendship toward them?! Then He adds: "You show them affection while they have disbelieved in what has come to you of the truth (Islam and the Quran), and they drive out the Messenger and yourselves because you believe in Allah, your Lord" (تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَ قَدْ كَفَرُوا بِما جاءَكُمْ مِنَ الْحَقِّ يُخْرِجُونَ الرَّسُولَ وَ إِيَّاكُمْ أَنْ تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ رَبِّكُمْ‌). [^2]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 269 (slightly summarized). This occasion of revelation has been narrated by Bukhari in his Sahih (Vol. 6, pp. 185-186), Fakhr al-Razi in his Tafsir, and also in the commentaries of "Ruh al-Ma'ani," "Ruh al-Bayan," "Fi Zilal," "Qurtubi," "Maraghi," and others with some variations.

[^2]: The phrase "تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ" is considered a circumstantial clause (hal) for the pronoun in "لا تَتَّخِذُوا" or an introductory sentence (isti'nafiyah) (Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 512). The "ba" in "بِالْمَوَدَّةِ" is either extra (za'idah) for emphasis, like in "وَ لا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ," or it is causal (sababiyah) with a deleted object, implying: "You deliver the news of the Prophet to them because of the affection that exists between you and them" (Ibid).

They both oppose you in belief and have risen in practical combat against you, and they have considered the act that is your greatest honor—namely, faith in the Lord—to be your greatest crime and sin, and for this reason, they have driven you out of your homes and homeland. Despite this, is it appropriate for you to show them affection and strive to save them from the clutches of divine punishment at the hands of the powerful warriors of the army of Islam?! Then, for further clarification, He adds: "If you have emigrated to strive in My way and seeking My pleasure, [do not establish bonds of friendship with them]" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهاداً فِي سَبِيلِي وَ ابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِي‌). [^1] If you truly claim to love Allah and have emigrated from your homes for His sake, and seek Jihad in the way of Allah and His pleasure, this matter is inconsistent with befriending the enemies of Allah.

Then, for more explanation, He adds: "You secretly maintain a relationship of affection with them, while I know best what you conceal and what you declare!" (تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَ أَنَا أَعْلَمُ بِما أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَ ما أَعْلَنْتُمْ‌). [^2] [^3]

[^1]: A group of commentators believe that this conditional sentence has a deleted apodosis (jaza) which is understood from the preceding sentence, implying: "If you have gone out to strive in My way and seeking My pleasure, do not take My enemies as allies."

[^2]: The above sentence is an introductory sentence (isti'nafiyah).

[^3]: The expression "بِما أَخْفَيْتُمْ‌" instead of "ma asrartum" is used because it conveys greater exaggeration, as ikhfa (concealment) is a deeper stage of secrecy (Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, under the verses discussed).

Therefore, what is the use of secrecy given Allah's knowledge of the unseen and the seen?

And at the end of the verse, as a decisive threat, He says: "And whoever of you does it has certainly strayed from the straight path" (وَ مَنْ يَفْعَلْهُ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ‌).

He has strayed from the path of knowing Allah by imagining that something remains hidden from Him, and from the path of faith, sincerity, and piety by establishing friendship with the enemies of Allah, and has struck at the root of his own life by informing his enemy of his secrets. This is the worst deviation that can happen to a believer after reaching the fountainhead of faith.

In the next verse, for further emphasis and clarification, He adds: Why do you establish friendship with them? While "If they gain dominance over you, they would be your enemies and extend their hands and their tongues against you with evil" (إِنْ يَثْقَفُوكُمْ يَكُونُوا لَكُمْ أَعْداءً وَ يَبْسُطُوا إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ وَ أَلْسِنَتَهُمْ بِالسُّوءِ). [^1] You feel compassion for them while their enmity toward you is so deep-rooted that if they get hold of you, they will spare no effort and will subject you to every kind of harm with their hands and tongues. Is compassion for such a group appropriate? And worst of all is that "they wish you would disbelieve" and lose your greatest honor, which is the jewel of faith (وَ وَدُّوا لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ‌).

[^1]: "يَثْقَفُوكُمْ‌" is from the root thaqaf and thaqafah, meaning skill in discerning or performing something; for this reason, it is also used to mean culture or skillful dominance over something.

And this is exactly the most painful blow they wish to inflict upon you.

In the last verse under discussion, He addresses the response of individuals like "Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah," who, in reply to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asking why he placed the secrets of the Muslims at the disposal of the polytheists of Mecca, said: "I have relatives and kin in Mecca who are trapped in the hands of the disbelievers; I fear they might harm them, so I wanted to protect them through this." He says: "Never will your relatives or your children benefit you" (لَنْ تَنْفَعَكُمْ أَرْحامُكُمْ وَ لا أَوْلادُكُمْ‌). For if children and relatives are without faith, they will be neither a source of honor and capital in this world nor a means of salvation in the hereafter. So why should believers do something for their sake that causes the wrath of Allah and severance from His allies?! Then He adds: "On the Day of Resurrection, He will judge between you" (يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ‌). [^1] The people of faith will go toward Paradise, and the people of disbelief toward Hell. This is, in fact, a reason for what was said before—meaning, when you are separated from one another and bonds are completely severed, what benefit will they have for you?! This verse is actually similar to what appears in verses 34-36 of Surah Abasa,

[^1]: Many commentators have considered "يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ" to be related to "يفصل," but some consider it related to "لَنْ تَنْفَعَكُمْ‌," and the result of both is close to each other. Although the first meaning seems more appropriate. It is also noteworthy that some have not interpreted "يفصل" as "separating," but rather from fasl meaning "judging and arbitrating," but the first meaning is more correct here.

where He says: يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ الْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ وَ أُمِّهِ وَ أَبِيهِ وَ صاحِبَتِهِ وَ بَنِيهِ‌:

"The Day a man will flee from his brother, and his mother and his father, and his wife and his children!" And at the end of the verse, He warns everyone once again that "Allah is Seeing of what you do" (وَ اللَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ).

He is aware of your intentions as well as the actions you perform in secret. And if in some cases He does not reveal your secrets, as with "Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah," it is due to certain wisdoms, not because He does not know or is unaware.

In truth, Allah's knowledge of the unseen and the seen, the secret and the manifest, is an effective means for the education of man—that he should see himself in His presence at all times, consider the entire world as the presence of the Truth, and be mindful of his speech, behavior, and even his intentions. This is why we say that the complete knowledge of Allah is the generative source of piety.

Ayahs

قَدْ كَانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَءَٰٓؤُا۟ مِنكُمْ وَمِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ كَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ وَبَدَا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةُ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءُ أَبَدًا حَتَّىٰ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ وَمَآ أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۖ رَّبَّنَا عَلَيْكَ تَوَكَّلْنَا وَإِلَيْكَ أَنَبْنَا وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤)
There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham and those who were with him, when they said to their own people, ‘Indeed we repudiate you and whatever you worship besides Allah. We disown you, and enmity and hate have appeared between you and us for ever, unless you come to have faith in Allah alone,’ apart from Abraham’s saying to his father, ‘I will surely plead forgiveness for you, though I cannot avail you anything against Allah.’ ‘Our Lord! In You do we put our trust, and to You do we turn penitently, and toward You is the destination. (4)
رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا رَبَّنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٥)
Our Lord! Do not make us a test for the faithless, and forgive us. Our Lord! Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (5)
لَقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِيهِمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٦)
There is certainly a good exemplar for you in them—for those who look forward to Allah and the Last Day—and anyone who refuses to comply [should know that] indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (6)

Commentary

Abraham Was an Exemplar for All of You

Since the Holy Quran, in many instances, provides significant role models from the history of humanity to complete its teachings, the verses under discussion—following the severe prohibition of befriending the enemies of God in the preceding verses—speak of Abraham عليه السلام and his mission. He is presented as a great leader respected by all nations, especially the Arabs. It states:

"There has already been for you an excellent pattern in Abraham and those with him"

(قَدْ كانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَ الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ‌) [^1] Abraham عليه السلام, the great Prophet whose entire life was a lesson in servitude and devotion to Allah, Jihad in the way of Allah, and love for His Pure Essence—Abraham, through whose blessed prayer the Islamic Ummah exists and in whose naming it takes pride—can serve as an excellent role model for you in this regard.

The phrase "and those with him" (وَ الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ‌) refers to the believers who accompanied him on this path, however few they were. The possibility that it refers to the prophets who echoed his call, or contemporary prophets—as some have suggested—seems unlikely. This is especially true because it is fitting here for the Quran to liken the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to "Abraham" and the Muslims to his companions and followers. Historical accounts also mention that a group in Babylon believed in him after witnessing his miracles and accompanied him in his migration toward Sham (the Levant), indicating he had loyal companions. [^2] Then, clarifying this meaning, it adds: "When they said to their people [the polytheists and idolaters]: 'Indeed, we are disassociated from you and from whatever you worship other than Allah!'" (إِذْ قالُوا لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَآؤُا مِنْكُمْ وَ مِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌).

We neither accept you nor your creed and religion; we detest both you and your worthless idols.

To emphasize further, they added: "We have denied you" (كَفَرْنا بِكُمْ‌).

Of course, this "denial" (kufr) is the "disavowal and dissociation" (kufr of bara'ah) mentioned in some narrations among the five types of kufr. [^1] For a third time, to provide even greater emphasis, they added: "And there has appeared between us and you animosity and hatred forever" (وَ بَدا بَيْنَنا وَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةُ وَ الْبَغْضاءُ أَبَداً).

And this state shall continue "until you believe in Allah alone" (حَتَّى تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَحْدَهُ‌).

In this manner, with utmost decisiveness and without any concealment, they declared their separation and dissociation from the enemies of Allah. They explicitly stated that this separation is irreversible and non-negotiable at any price, and it will continue forever unless they change their course and turn from the path of disbelief to the path of faith.

However, since this general and universal law in the life of Abraham had an exception—which was made for the sake of guiding some of the polytheists—it follows by saying: They severed all ties with the disbelieving people and spoke no words of affection to them, "except for the saying of Abraham to his father [his uncle Azar]: 'I will surely ask forgiveness for you, but I do not possess [power to do] for you anything against Allah [and forgiveness is only in His hand]'" (إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ وَ ما أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ). [^2] This is, in fact, an exception to the issue of Abraham and his companions severing all ties with the idolaters, which itself had specific conditions and wisdom. Evidence suggests that Abraham likely observed a readiness for faith in his uncle Azar, but Azar was concerned about what would happen to his past period of idolatry if he embraced monotheism. Abraham promised him that he would seek forgiveness for him before Allah, and he fulfilled this promise. However, Azar did not believe, and when it became clear to Abraham that he was an enemy of Allah and would never believe, he ceased seeking forgiveness for him and severed ties with him.

Since the Muslims were generally aware of this interaction between Abraham and Azar, it was possible that this could become an excuse for individuals like "Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah" to establish secret ties with the disbelievers. Thus, the Quran explains that this exception occurred under specific circumstances and was a means to draw Azar toward faith, not for worldly goals. Therefore, in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 114, it says: "And the request of forgiveness of Abraham for his father was only because of a promise he had made to him. But when it became apparent to him that he was an enemy to Allah, he disassociated himself from him. Indeed Abraham was compassionate and patient."

However, a group of commentators have considered this an exception to "Abraham being an exemplar," stating that he should be emulated in everything except his seeking forgiveness for his uncle Azar.

Although this interpretation appears in the words of several commentators, it seems very unlikely. Firstly, he was an "exemplar" in everything, even in this action; for if the same conditions as Azar's were found in some polytheists, showing affection to them to attract them toward faith would be a good deed. Secondly, Abraham عليه السلام was an infallible Prophet, one of the great and striving messengers, and all his actions are a model; it makes no sense to except this matter.

In short, Abraham and his followers were strongly opposed to the idolaters, and this lesson must be taken as a model from them. The story of Azar had specific conditions which, if they arise for us, are also worthy of emulation. [^1] Since struggling against the enemies of Allah with such clarity and decisiveness—especially when they possess outward power—is not possible except through reliance on the Essence of Allah, the end of the verse adds: They said: "Our Lord, upon You we have relied, and to You we have returned, and to You is the destination" (رَبَّنا عَلَيْكَ تَوَكَّلْنا وَ إِلَيْكَ أَنَبْنا وَ إِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ).

In truth, they presented three matters to the Divine Presence in this phrase: First, reliance (Tawakkul) on His Essence; second, repentance and returning to Him; and third, attention to the reality that the final return of everything is to Him. These are cause and effect for one another: belief in the Resurrection and the final return leads to repentance, and repentance revives the spirit of reliance within man. [^2]

In the following verse, it mentions another request of Abraham and his companions, which is sensitive and striking in this context: "Our Lord, make us not [objects of] trial for the disbelievers" (رَبَّنا لا تَجْعَلْنا فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

This expression might refer to actions like those of "Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah," which sometimes stem from unaware individuals who do something that strengthens the misguided while thinking they have done nothing wrong.

Or it may refer to: "Do not let us fall into their clutches, and do not let us be defeated by them, lest they say: 'If these people were on the truth, they would never have suffered defeat,' and this becomes a cause for their further misguidance."

That is, if they fear defeat and the dominance of the disbelievers, it is not for their own sakes, but rather so that the religion of truth is not called into question, and the outward victory of the polytheists is not taken as proof of their correctness. This is the way of a true believer: whatever he wants, he wants for Allah; he has severed ties with all else and joined with Allah, seeking everything for His pleasure.

We turned our faces toward You and tied our hearts to You, We came back from everyone else and sat with You. Whatever was not a bond with the Beloved, we severed, Whatever was not a covenant with the Friend, we broke.

At the end of the verse, it adds: "And forgive us, our Lord" (وَ اغْفِرْ لَنا رَبَّنا). "Indeed, it is You who is the Exalted in Might, the Wise" (إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ‌). Your power is invincible, and Your wisdom is pervasive in all things.

This sentence may imply that if, during our lives, there was any sign of inclination, affection, or friendship toward Your enemies, forgive us for this slip. This is a lesson for Muslims to also take as a model: if a "Hatib" is found among them, they should seek forgiveness and return to Allah.

In the final verse under discussion, it emphasizes once more the same point emphasized in the first verse, stating: "There has certainly been for you in them an excellent pattern for anyone whose hope is in Allah and the Last Day" (لَقَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ فِيهِمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا اللَّهَ وَ الْيَوْمَ الْآخِرَ). [^1] Not only their dissociation from the idolaters and the path of disbelief, but also their prayers and requests before Allah—examples of which were seen in the previous verses—are a model for all Muslims.

This model is taken only by those who have tied their hearts to Allah, whose faith in the Origin and the Resurrection has illuminated their hearts, and who have set out on the path of truth.

Undoubtedly, the benefit of this emulation and following returns to the Muslims themselves before anyone else. Therefore, it adds at the end: "And whoever turns away [and plots friendship with the enemies of Allah]—then indeed, Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy" (وَ مَنْ يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ).

For plotting friendship with the enemies of Allah strengthens them, and their strength causes your own defeat; and if they gain mastery over you, they will show mercy to neither the young nor the old. [^2]

Key Points

1- Eternal Role Models

Practical programs are always the most effective, because action reflects a person's deep faith in their words, and a word that comes from the heart inevitably settles in the heart.

The existence of great role models and exemplars in human life has always been an effective means for their education. For this reason, the Prophet and the infallible leaders demonstrated the most important branch of guidance through their own actions. Thus, when "Sunnah" is discussed, it is said that Sunnah consists of the "speech," "action," and "tacit approval" of the Infallible (Ma'sum). That is, the speech, action, and silence of the infallible leaders are all proofs and guides. It is also for this reason that infallibility (Ismah) is a condition for all Prophets and Imams, so they may be role models in all fields.

The Quran also affirms this important matter, introducing role models and exemplars in all fields for the believers. Among these, in the verses under discussion, it speaks twice of Abraham and his companions, and in Surah Al-Ahzab, it introduces the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a pattern and exemplar (Uswah) for the Muslims.

(It should be noted that "Uswah" has a gerundial meaning, referring to the act of emulating and practical following, although in everyday Persian usage, it refers to the person who is emulated).

In the perilous Battle of the Trench (Ahzab), during the days when the Muslims were severely tested and crushing events made even the strongest individuals tremble, Allah introduced the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a pattern and exemplar of steadfastness, perseverance, faith, sincerity, and calmness amidst the storm. Of course, this was not limited to the field of "Ahzab"; the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was a great role model for Muslims everywhere.

The slogan: "Be callers of people [to God] by your actions and do not be callers by your tongues," which is taken from a Hadith of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [^1], is proof that all true Muslims must, in turn, be role models and exemplars for others. They should introduce Islam to the world through the language of action; had this been done, Islam would have encompassed the world!

2- Allah is Independent of All

In the Holy Quran, it is repeatedly emphasized that if Allah gives you commands—which may sometimes seem difficult or taxing—do not forget that all their benefits return to you. For the boundless ocean of God's existence has no deficiency to seek help from you; moreover, you have nothing to give Him, rather everything you have is from Him.

In the Hadith Qudsi, it is stated: "O My servants! You can never reach [a state of] harming Me nor can you reach [a state of] benefiting Me. O My servants! If the first of you and the last of you, and the humans among you and the jinn among you, were to have the heart of the most God-fearing man among you, that would not increase My kingdom in anything. And if the first of you and the last of you, and the humans among you and the jinn among you, were to have the heart of the most wicked man among you, that would not decrease My kingdom in anything."

"O My servants! If the first of you and the last of you, and the humans among you and the jinn among you, were to stand in one place and ask of Me, and I were to give every person what he asked, that would not decrease what I have, any more than a needle decreases the sea if it is put into it."

"O My servants! It is but your deeds that I reckon up for you and then I recompense you for them. So let him who finds good praise Allah, and let him who finds other than that blame no one but himself." [^1]

3- The Fundamental Principle of "Love for Allah and Hate for Allah"

Ideological bonding is the most important bond that connects human beings to one another, and every other bond is overshadowed by it.

This is a point that the Quran emphasizes repeatedly. If this bond is affected by relationships of friendship, kinship, or personal interests, the pillars of religion will be shaken.

Furthermore, ultimate value lies in faith and piety; how is it possible to establish [deep] connections with those who lack these two?

Thus, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Whoever loves for Allah, hates for Allah, and gives for the sake of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is among those whose faith is complete." [^2] In another Hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read: "Among the firmest handholds of faith is that you love for Allah, hate for Allah, give for Allah, and withhold for Allah." [^1] Narrations in this regard are very numerous; for more information, refer to the second volume of Usul al-Kafi, "Chapter on Love for Allah." The late Kulayni has narrated 16 Hadiths in this chapter on this subject.

Also, for further explanation regarding "Love for Allah and Hate for Allah," refer to Volume 23 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 22 of Surah Al-Mujadilah.

Ayahs

۞ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَجْعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ عَادَيْتُم مِّنْهُم مَّوَدَّةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ قَدِيرٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧)
It may be that Allah will bring about affection between you and those with whom you are at enmity, and Allah is all-powerful, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (7)
لَّا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَلَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَتُقْسِطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ (٨)
Allah does not forbid you from dealing with kindness and justice with those [polytheists] who did not make war against you on account of religion and did not expel you from your homes. Indeed Allah loves the just. (8)
إِنَّمَا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَأَخْرَجُوكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ وَظَـٰهَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْرَاجِكُمْ أَن تَوَلَّوْهُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٩)
Allah forbids you only in regard to those who made war against you on account of religion and expelled you from your homes and supported [the polytheists of Makkah] in your expulsion, that you make friends with them, and whoever makes friends with them—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (9)

Commentary

Love for Disbelievers Who Are Not at War

In these verses, the discussions from previous verses regarding "Love for the sake of Allah and Hatred for the sake of Allah" and the severance of ties with the polytheists continue. Since this severance of ties created a kind of emotional vacuum for a group of Muslims—despite the fact that the true believers and companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) showed steadfastness in this path—Allah gives them glad tidings to reward them and fill this void. He tells them not to grieve, for the situation will not remain as it is:

"It may be that Allah will bring about affection between you and those of them who are your enemies (through their acceptance of Islam)" (عَسَى اللَّهُ أَنْ يَجْعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ بَيْنَ الَّذِينَ عادَيْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ مَوَدَّةً).

And this matter eventually came to pass; the eighth year of the Hijra arrived, Mecca was conquered, and the people of Mecca entered the religion of Allah in crowds (يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ أَفْواجاً). The dark clouds of enmity and malice cleared from the sky of their lives, and the sun of faith began to shine with the warmth of love and friendship.

Some commentators consider this sentence to be an allusion to the marriage of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) with "Umm Habiba," the daughter of Abu Sufyan. She had embraced Islam and had gone to Abyssinia with the migrants along with her husband, Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh [1]. Her husband died there, and the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) sent someone to the Negus (Najashi) to propose marriage to her on his behalf. Since the bond of son-in-lawship among the Arabs was a cause for the reduction of animosities, this matter had an effect on Abu Sufyan and the people of Mecca.

However, this possibility seems unlikely, because the verses under discussion were revealed on the eve of the Conquest of Mecca, as "Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah" intended to inform the polytheists of Mecca of this event through his letter. Meanwhile, we know that Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and his companions returned to Medina some time before this event (coinciding with the Conquest of Khaybar) [1]. In any case, if those whom the Muslims love separate from their ideological path, one should not despair of their return, for Allah is capable of all things. It is He who can transform hearts, and it is He who forgives the sins and errors of His servants. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He adds: "And Allah is All-Powerful, and Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful" (وَ اللَّهُ قَدِيرٌ وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

The word "Asa" (عسى) in the Arabic language is used in cases where there is hope for something to happen. Since this meaning is sometimes associated with "ignorance" or "incapacity," many commentators have interpreted it in the Holy Quran as the hope of others from Allah. However, as we have said previously, there is no obstacle to this word retaining its original meaning in the speech of Allah, because sometimes certain conditions are necessary to reach a goal; whenever some conditions are not yet met, this expression is used (reflect on this).

[1] Ubaydullah ibn Jahsh is the brother of Abdullah ibn Jahsh. Unfortunately, Ubaydullah did not remain in Islam and chose Christianity in Abyssinia; for this reason, Umm Habiba separated from him. However, his brother Abdullah remained in Islam, was among the warriors of Uhud, and drank the nectar of martyrdom on that battlefield. [1] A summary of this event has been narrated by many commentators, but for a detailed account, please refer to "Usd al-Ghabah fi Ma'rifat al-Sahabah," Vol. 5, p. 573.

The subsequent verses are an explanation and clarification regarding the issue of severing friendly ties with the polytheists. He says: "Allah does not forbid you from being kind and acting justly toward those who did not fight you because of religion and did not expel you from your homes" (لا يَنْهاكُمُ اللَّهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَ لَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ أَنْ تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَ تُقْسِطُوا إِلَيْهِمْ).

"Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ).

"Allah only forbids you from befriending those who fought you because of religion and expelled you from your homes or aided in your expulsion. Yes, Allah forbids you from any bond of friendship with them" (إِنَّما يَنْهاكُمُ اللَّهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ قاتَلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَ أَخْرَجُوكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ وَ ظاهَرُوا عَلى‌ إِخْراجِكُمْ أَنْ تَوَلَّوْهُمْ). "And whoever befriends them—it is they who are the wrongdoers" (وَ مَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ).

In this way, non-Muslim individuals are divided into two groups: The first group are those who stood against the Muslims, drew swords against them, and forcibly expelled them from their homes and dwellings—in short, they openly showed their enmity and hostility toward Islam and Muslims in word and deed. The duty of Muslims is to cut off all interaction with this group and refrain from any bond of affection and friendship. A clear example of this were the polytheists of Mecca, especially the leaders of the Quraysh; some officially took part in this, and others aided them.

However, there was another group who, despite their disbelief and polytheism, had nothing to do with the Muslims. They neither harbored enmity, nor fought them, nor took action to expel them from their city and land. Some of them had even entered into non-aggression pacts with the Muslims. Being kind to this group and showing them affection was not prohibited, and if a treaty had been made with them, it had to be honored, and justice had to be pursued in its implementation.

An example of this group was the tribe of "Khuza'ah," who had a non-aggression pact with the Muslims.

Therefore, there remains no room for the statement of a group of commentators who considered this instruction to be abrogated, citing verse 5 of Surah At-Tawbah as the abrogator, which says: "When the sacred months have passed, kill the polytheists wherever you find them." This is because that verse of Surah At-Tawbah speaks only of the polytheists who broke their treaties and rose in open opposition against Islam and Muslims, as evidenced by the verses following it [1]. Some commentators have narrated regarding this verse that the divorced wife of Abu Bakr brought gifts from Mecca for her daughter "Asma." Asma refused to accept them and did not even allow her mother to enter. The above verse was revealed, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) commanded her to welcome her mother, accept her gift, and treat her with honor and kindness [2]. This narration shows that not all the people of Mecca were subject to this ruling, but rather there was a minority among them who did not hold a hostile position against the Muslims.

In any case, from these verses, a "general and fundamental principle" regarding the nature of the relationship between Muslims and non-Muslims is derived. This is constant not only for that time but also for today and tomorrow: Muslims are duty-bound to stand firmly against any group, population, or country that takes a hostile stance against them and rises against Islam and Muslims, or aids the enemies of Islam, and they must sever any bond of affection and friendship with them.

However, if they remain neutral toward Islam and Muslims despite being disbelievers, or if they are inclined toward peace, Muslims can establish friendly relations with them—though not to the extent they have with their Muslim brothers, and not to the extent that leads to their influence among the Muslims.

And if a population or government belongs to the first group and changes its stance, or conversely, belongs to the second group and changes its path, their current situation must be the criterion, and they should be dealt with according to the above verses.

[1] Some commentators have suggested that the permission for friendship might be regarding believers who had accepted Islam but remained in Mecca and had not migrated. However, the tone of the verses clearly shows that all these discussions are about non-Muslims. [2] Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 481—This narration also appears in Sahih Bukhari and many books of Tafsir with some variations.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا جَآءَكُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ مُهَـٰجِرَٰتٍ فَٱمْتَحِنُوهُنَّ ۖ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَـٰنِهِنَّ ۖ فَإِنْ عَلِمْتُمُوهُنَّ مُؤْمِنَـٰتٍ فَلَا تَرْجِعُوهُنَّ إِلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ ۖ لَا هُنَّ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحِلُّونَ لَهُنَّ ۖ وَءَاتُوهُم مَّآ أَنفَقُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ أَن تَنكِحُوهُنَّ إِذَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ ۚ وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا۟ بِعِصَمِ ٱلْكَوَافِرِ وَسْـَٔلُوا۟ مَآ أَنفَقْتُمْ وَلْيَسْـَٔلُوا۟ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ حُكْمُ ٱللَّهِ ۖ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (١٠)
O you who have faith! When faithful women come to you as immigrants, test them. Allah knows best [the state of] their faith. Then, if you ascertain them to be [genuinely] faithful women, do not send them back to the faithless. They are not lawful for them, nor are they lawful for them, but give them what [dowry] they have spent [for them]. There is no sin upon you in marrying them when you have given them their dowries. Do not hold on to [conjugal] ties with faithless women. Demand [from the infidels] what you have spent [as dowries], and let the faithless demand [from you] what they have spent [as dowries]. That is Allah’s judgment; He judges between you, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (10)
وَإِن فَاتَكُمْ شَىْءٌ مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْكُفَّارِ فَعَاقَبْتُمْ فَـَٔاتُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ذَهَبَتْ أَزْوَٰجُهُم مِّثْلَ مَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ (١١)
If anything [of the dowries] pertaining to your wives is not reclaimed from the faithless and then you have your turn, then give to those whose wives have left the like of what they have spent, and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith. (11)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for these verses that: The Messenger of Allah ﷺ signed a treaty with the polytheists of Mecca at Hudaybiyyah. One of the clauses of the treaty was that anyone from the people of Mecca who joined the Muslims must be returned, but if any of the Muslims abandoned Islam and returned to Mecca, they (the polytheists) were not required to return them.

At this time, a woman named "Subay'ah" accepted Islam and joined the Muslims in the land of Hudaybiyyah. Her husband came to the Prophet ﷺ and said: "O Muhammad! Return my wife to me, for this is one of the clauses of our treaty, and its ink has not yet dried." The above verse was revealed and commanded that the migrating women be tested (Ibn Abbas says their test was that they had to swear an oath that their migration was not due to hatred for their husband, or a desire for a new land, or a worldly goal, but solely for the sake of Islam).

The woman swore an oath that it was so. Thereupon, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ paid the husband the dower (mahr) he had given and the expenses he had incurred, and said: "According to this clause of the contract, only men are to be returned, not women." [^1]

Commentary

Compensating the Losses of Muslims and Disbelievers

In the previous verses, the discussion was about "hatred for the sake of Allah" and severing ties with the enemies of God. However, in the verses under discussion, the talk is about "love for the sake of Allah" and establishing ties with those who separate themselves from disbelief and join the faith.

In the first verse, it speaks of migrating women, and a total of seven commands are mentioned in this verse, mainly concerning migrating women, and partly concerning disbelieving women.

1- The first command concerns the testing of "migrating women." Addressing the believers, He says: "O you who have believed! When believing women come to you as emigrants, do not drive them away, but examine them" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا جَاءَكُمُ الْمُؤْمِنَاتُ مُهَاجِرَاتٍ فَامْتَحِنُوهُنَّ).

The command to examine them, despite calling them "believers," is because they outwardly recited the Shahadatayn (the two testimonies of faith) and were among the ranks of the people of faith, but the examination was to ensure that this outward appearance harmonized with their inner state.

As we mentioned, the method of this examination was to have them swear by Allah that their migration was for no reason other than accepting Islam, and they had to swear that they did not migrate due to enmity with a husband, or desire for another man, or love for the land of Medina and the like.

It is also possible that the twelfth verse of this same Surah serves as an explanation of the nature of the examination of migrating women—that (according to it) they must pledge allegiance (bay'ah) to the Prophet of Islam ﷺ that they will not follow the path of polytheism, nor approach theft, immoral acts, killing their children, and the like, and that they be entirely submissive to the command of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

Of course, it is possible that some might lie in that oath or this pledge, but the commitment of many people—even the polytheists at that time—to the issue of allegiance and swearing by Allah caused individuals to lie less. Thus, although the said examination was not always a definitive proof of their true faith, it could often reflect the reality.

Therefore, in the next sentence, He adds: "Allah knows best as to [the state of] their faith" (اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَانِهِنَّ).

2- In the next command, He says: "And if you know them to be true believers, do not return them to the disbelievers" (فَإِنْ عَلِمْتُمُوهُنَّ مُؤْمِنَاتٍ فَلَا تَرْجِعُوهُنَّ إِلَى الْكُفَّارِ).

It is true that one of the imposed clauses of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was to return individuals who migrated from Mecca to Medina as Muslims back to Mecca, but this clause did not include women. Therefore, the Prophet ﷺ never returned them to the disbelievers—an act which, if performed, would have been extremely dangerous given the extreme vulnerability of women in that society.

3- In the third stage, which is actually a reason for the previous ruling, He adds: "They are not lawful [wives] for them, nor are they [the disbelievers] lawful [husbands] for them" (لَا هُنَّ حِلٌّ لَهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحِلُّونَ لَهُنَّ).

It must be so, because faith and disbelief cannot coexist, and the sacred bond of marriage cannot establish a relationship between a believer and a disbeliever, for they are on two opposite paths, whereas the marriage contract must establish a kind of unity between the two spouses, and these two are incompatible.

Of course, at the beginning of Islam, when the Islamic society had not yet been established, there were couples where one was a disbeliever and the other a Muslim, and the Prophet did not forbid it until Islam took root. However, apparently after the Peace of Hudaybiyyah, the command for complete separation was given, and the verse under discussion is one of the proofs for this matter.

4- Since it was the custom of the Arabs to pay the dower of their wives in advance, in the fourth command He adds: "And give them [their disbelieving husbands] what they have spent [on them]" (وَآتُوهُمْ مَا أَنْفَقُوا).

It is true that their husbands are disbelievers, but since the move toward separation began from the wife's side through faith, Islamic justice requires that the husband's losses be compensated.

Does "spending" (infaq) here refer only to the dower, or does it include other expenses incurred in this path? Most commentators have chosen the first meaning, and this is the certain minimum from the verse, although some like "Abu al-Futuh al-Razi" in his commentary have also mentioned other expenditures. [^2] Of course, this payment of dower was in the case of polytheists who had signed a non-aggression pact with the Muslims at Hudaybiyyah or elsewhere.

But who should pay this dower? It appears that this task is the responsibility of the Islamic government and the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal), because all matters that do not have a specific person responsible in the Islamic society fall upon the government, and the plural address in the verse under discussion is evidence of this meaning (just as is seen in the verses regarding the punishment for a thief or an adulterer).

5- Another ruling that follows the above rulings is where He says:

"And there is no blame upon you if you marry them when you have given them their dowers" (وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْ تَنْكِحُوهُنَّ إِذَا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ).

Lest you imagine that because they previously received a dower from a former husband and its equivalent was paid to their husband from the public treasury, now that you marry them, there is no dower involved and it is free for you! No, the dignity of the woman requires that in the new marriage, a suitable dower be considered for her as well.

It should be noted that here the woman is separated from the disbelieving husband without a divorce, but she must observe the waiting period ('iddah).

The famous jurist, the author of Jawahir al-Kalam, in explaining the words of the researcher in Shara'i'—who said: "In cases other than a man and woman who are People of the Book, whenever one of the two spouses accepts Islam, if it is before consummation, the contract is immediately annulled, and if it is after consummation, it is contingent upon the passing of the waiting period"—states: "There is no disagreement whatsoever regarding these rulings, and the narrations and fatwas of the jurists are in harmony on this matter." [^3]

6- However, if the situation is reversed—that is, the husband accepts Islam and the wife remains in disbelief—here too the marital relationship is severed and the marriage is annulled, as He says in the continuation of this verse: "And hold not to the marriage bonds of disbelieving women" (وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا بِعِصَمِ الْكَوَافِرِ).

"Isam" (عصم) is the plural of "Ismah" (عصمت), which originally means "prevention" or "protection," and here—as stated and as the context testifies—it means marriage and matrimony (of course, some have specified that it refers to permanent marriage, and the expression "Ismah" is appropriate for this meaning because it prevents the woman from marrying any other person permanently).

"Kawafir" (كوافر) is the plural of "Kafirah" (كافرة), meaning disbelieving women.

Whether this ruling is specific to polytheistic women or includes the People of the Book, such as Christian and Jewish women, is a matter of debate among jurists, and the narrations in this regard vary, the details of which should be studied in books of jurisprudence. However, the outward meaning of the verse is absolute and includes all disbelieving women and is not limited by its occasion of revelation. But the issue of the waiting period is established here a fortiori, because if a child is born to that woman, the child is a Muslim since the father was a Muslim.

7- In the final ruling mentioned in the verse, the talk is about the dower of women who separate from Islam and join the people of disbelief. He says: "And ask for what you have spent, and let them ask for what they have spent" (وَاسْأَلُوا مَا أَنْفَقْتُمْ وَلْيَسْأَلُوا مَا أَنْفَقُوا).

And this is the requirement of justice and respect for mutual rights.

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis on what has passed, He says: "That is the judgment of Allah; He judges between you. And Allah is Knowing and Wise" (ذَلِكُمْ حُكْمُ اللَّهِ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ).

These are rulings that all stem from Divine knowledge and are infused with wisdom, and the rights of all individuals are considered therein, and they are completely consistent with the principle of Islamic justice and equity. Awareness of the fact that all of this is from Allah is considered the greatest guarantee for the implementation of these rulings.

In the second and final verse under discussion, continuing this discourse, He says: "And if any of your wives have gone from you to the disbelievers [by apostatizing], and then you have your turn [in combat and gain spoils], then give to those whose wives have gone the equivalent of what they had spent" (وَإِنْ فَاتَكُمْ شَيْءٌ مِنْ أَزْوَاجِكُمْ إِلَى الْكُفَّارِ فَعَاقَبْتُمْ فَآتُوا الَّذِينَ ذَهَبَتْ أَزْوَاجُهُمْ مِثْلَ مَا أَنْفَقُوا).

According to the previous verse, Muslims could collect the dower of such women from the disbelievers, just as they had the right to receive the dower of their wives who joined Islam and migrated to Medina from the Muslims.

However, according to some narrations, while the Muslims acted upon this just ruling, the polytheists of Mecca refused. Therefore, it was commanded that in order not to violate the rights of these individuals, whenever spoils were obtained, their rights should be paid first before the spoils are divided.

It is also possible that the above ruling pertains to groups with whom the Muslims had no treaty, and naturally, they were not willing to return the dower of such women to the Muslims. Combining both meanings is also possible. "Aqabtum" (عاقبتم) is from the root "Mu'aqabah" (معاقبة), originally from "Aqib" (عقب) meaning "heel," and for this reason, the word "Uqba" (عقبى) means "recompense" and "Uqubat" (عقوبت) means punishment for a wrong act. For this reason, "Mu'aqabah" is used to mean punishing and retaliating, and sometimes this word is used to mean "alternation" in a matter, because individuals who perform a task alternately each arrive behind the other.

Therefore, "Aqabtum" in the above verse has been interpreted as the Muslims' victory over the disbelievers and their punishment and penalty, and incidentally taking spoils, and also as "alternation" because one day is the turn of the disbelievers and another day is the turn of the Muslims when they prevail over them.

It has also been suggested that this phrase refers to reaching the "consequence" or end of the matter, and the end of the matter here means obtaining the spoils of war.

Whichever of these meanings it may be, the result is the same; only the paths to reaching this result are mentioned differently (take note).

At the end of the verse, He invites all Muslims to piety (taqwa), saying: "And fear Allah, in whom you are believers" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ).

The command to piety here may be because usually, in determining the amount of the dower, the word of the husbands is relied upon, as there is no way to prove it other than their own word, and it is possible that satanic whispers might cause them to claim more than the actual amount; therefore, He advises them to have piety.

It is mentioned in histories and narrations that this Islamic ruling applied only to six women who broke away from their Muslim husbands and joined the disbelievers, and the Prophet ﷺ returned the dower of all of them to their husbands from the spoils of war.

Key Point: Justice Even Toward Enemies

Reflecting on the above verses and the elegance, subtlety, and specific points of discourse used in the said rulings shows to what extent the principle of justice and equity has been considered in the legislation of Islamic rulings, such that even in critical and stormy times, efforts are made so that no loss or harm reaches anyone, even the disbelievers.

This is while the norm of the world is that in crises, everyone says the conditions are extraordinary and must be submitted to, and it is not the place for fulfilling rights, and any kind of disorder must be tolerated. The Quran says that even in the most difficult conditions, one must strive so that no right is lost—not only for friends, but if enemies also have a right, it must be observed.

These kinds of Islamic rulings are a type of miracle and a sign of the truthfulness of the Prophet's ﷺ call, that in such an environment where neither property had respect nor life had value, he exerts such effort in fulfilling rights.

[^1]: The above occasion of revelation is mentioned in many books of commentary, and we have brought it above from Majma' al-Bayan with some summary and adaptation. "Tabarsi" narrated this hadith from "Ibn Abbas." [^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 11, p. 126. [^3]: Jawahir al-Kalam, Vol. 30, p. 54.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِذَا جَآءَكَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ يُبَايِعْنَكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن لَّا يُشْرِكْنَ بِٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَسْرِقْنَ وَلَا يَزْنِينَ وَلَا يَقْتُلْنَ أَوْلَـٰدَهُنَّ وَلَا يَأْتِينَ بِبُهْتَـٰنٍ يَفْتَرِينَهُۥ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِنَّ وَأَرْجُلِهِنَّ وَلَا يَعْصِينَكَ فِى مَعْرُوفٍ ۙ فَبَايِعْهُنَّ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُنَّ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢)
O Prophet! If faithful women come to you, to take the oath of allegiance to you, [pledging] that they shall not ascribe any partners to Allah, that they shall not steal, nor commit adultery, nor kill their children, nor utter any slander that they may have intentionally fabricated, nor disobey you in what is right, then accept their allegiance, and plead for them to Allah for forgiveness. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful (12)

Commentary

Conditions for the Allegiance of Women

Following the previous verses which explained the rulings concerning migrating women, the first verse under discussion describes the ruling for the pledge of allegiance (bay'ah) of women to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله.

As commentators have written, this verse was revealed on the day of the Conquest of Makkah. When the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was stationed on Mount Safa, He took the pledge of allegiance from the men. The women of Makkah who had believed came to Him to pledge their allegiance, and the above verse was revealed, explaining the manner of the pledge with them.

Addressing the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, He says: "O Prophet! When the believing women come to you to give you their pledge—that they will not associate anything with Allah, that they will not steal, that they will not commit adultery, that they will not kill their children, that they will not bring forth a slander they have fabricated between their hands and feet, and that they will not disobey you in any righteous matter—then accept their pledge and ask Allah to forgive them. Indeed, Allah is All-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِذَا جَاءَكَ الْمُؤْمِنَاتُ يُبَايِعْنَكَ عَلَىٰ أَنْ لَا يُشْرِكْنَ بِاللَّهِ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَسْرِقْنَ وَلَا يَزْنِينَ وَلَا يَقْتُلْنَ أَوْلَادَهُنَّ وَلَا يَأْتِينَ بِبُهْتَانٍ يَفْتَرِينَهُ بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِنَّ وَأَرْجُلِهِنَّ وَلَا يَعْصِينَكَ فِي مَعْرُوفٍ فَبَايِعْهُنَّ وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَهُنَّ اللَّهَ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ).

Following this event, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله took their pledge.

Regarding the manner of the pledge, some have written that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله ordered a vessel of water to be brought; He placed His hand in the vessel, and the women placed their hands in the other side of the vessel. Others have said that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله took their pledge over their clothing.

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, six conditions are mentioned for the women's pledge, all of which they must accept:

1- Abandoning all forms of polytheism and idolatry—this condition is the foundation of Islam and faith.

2- Abandoning theft—this perhaps primarily refers to the husband's property, because the poor financial situation of that time, the stinginess of men, and the low level of culture caused women to steal from their husbands' property, possibly giving it to their relatives. The story of Hind, which will follow, also bears witness to this meaning; however, the concept of the verse is broad and general.

3- Abandoning the defilement of adultery, as history records that in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), deviation from the path of chastity was very prevalent.

4- Not killing children, which was done in two ways: sometimes as "abortion" and sometimes as "wa’d" (burying daughters and sons alive)!

5- Abandoning slander and fabrication—some have interpreted this as taking abandoned children from the roadside and claiming that the child belonged to their husband (this was more possible during the husband's long absences). Some have also considered it an allusion to a shameful practice, also a remnant of the Age of Ignorance, where a woman would place herself at the disposal of several men, and when a child was born to her, she would attribute the child to whichever of them she desired. However, considering that the issue of adultery was mentioned previously and the continuation of such a practice was impossible in Islam, this interpretation seems unlikely, and the first interpretation is more appropriate, although the breadth of the verse's concept includes every kind of slander and fabrication. The expression "between their hands and feet" (بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِنَّ وَأَرْجُلِهِنَّ) may refer to those abandoned children who were placed in their laps during breastfeeding and were naturally before their feet and hands.

6- Not disobeying the constructive commands of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. This ruling is also broad and includes all commands of the Prophet, although some have considered it an allusion to certain practices of women in the Age of Ignorance, such as loud wailing over the dead, tearing garments, and scratching the face and the like; however, it is not limited to these.

Here, the question arises: why was the pledge with women conditional upon these terms, while the pledge with men was only upon Islam and Jihad?

In response, it can be said: what was most important regarding men in that environment was faith and Jihad. Regarding women, since Jihad was not prescribed for them, other conditions were mentioned which, after Monotheism (Tawhid), were the most important matters in which women in that deviated society were entangled.

Key Points

1- The Relationship of Women's Pledge with Their Islamic Personality

In the commentary of Surah Al-Fath (under verse 18), we had a detailed discussion regarding the pledge (bay'ah), its conditions, and its characteristics in Islam, which we see no need to repeat here [1]. What is necessary to remind here is the issue of the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله pledge with women, and that too with the beneficial and constructive conditions mentioned in the above verse.

This issue shows that, contrary to the claims of the ignorant or the biased who say Islam has not valued half of human society—namely women—and has not taken them into account, it has precisely accounted for them in the most important matters. Among these is the issue of "pledge," which took place once at Hudaybiyyah (in the sixth year of Hijrah) and once at the Conquest of Makkah. They entered into this divine covenant side-by-side with men, and even accepted more conditions than the men—conditions that revived the human identity of the woman and saved her from being turned into a worthless commodity or a means for the pleasure-seeking of lustful men.

2- The Story of the Pledge of Hind, the Wife of Abu Sufyan

During the Conquest of Makkah, among the women who came and pledged allegiance to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was "Hind," the wife of Abu Sufyan—a woman of whom Islamic history remembers painful events, including the martyrdom of Hamzah, the Master of Martyrs, on the field of Uhud in that tragic manner.

Although she was eventually forced to kneel before Islam and the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and ostensibly become a Muslim, the story of her pledge shows that she remained essentially loyal to her former beliefs. Therefore, it is not surprising that the Umayyad dynasty and her descendants committed such unprecedented crimes after the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله.

In any case, commentators have written that Hind wore a veil over her face and came to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله while He was on Mount Safa, with a group of women with her. When the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said, "I take your pledge, O women, that you shall not associate anything with Allah," Hind objected and said: "You take a commitment from us that you did not take from the men" (because on that day, the men's pledge was only upon faith and Jihad).

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, without paying attention to her words, continued and said: "And do not steal." Hind said: "Abu Sufyan is a stingy man, and I have taken things from his property; I do not know if he forgives me or not." Abu Sufyan was present and said: "Whatever you have taken from my property in the past, I have made it all lawful for you (but be careful in the future)!"

It was here that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله laughed and recognized Hind, saying: "Is it you, Hind?" She said: "Yes, O Prophet of Allah! Forgive the past, may Allah forgive you!"

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله continued: "And do not commit adultery." Hind said out of surprise: "Does a free woman ever commit such an act?!" Some of those present who knew her situation in the Age of Ignorance laughed at this statement, as Hind's past was hidden from no one.

Again, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله continued and said: "And do not kill your children." Hind said: "We raised them when they were small, but when they grew up, you killed them! You and they know best" (referring to her son "Hanzalah" who had been killed at the hands of Ali عليه السلام on the day of Badr).

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله smiled at this remark, and when He reached the phrase: "Do not commit slander and fabrication," Hind added: "Slander is ugly, and you do not invite us except to righteousness, goodness, and noble character."

And when He said: "You must obey my command in all good deeds," Hind added: "We have not sat here intending in our hearts to disobey you" (while certainly the matter was not so, but according to the teachings of Islam, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was duty-bound to accept these declarations) [1].

3- Obedience in What is Right (Ma'ruf)

One of the interesting points derived from the above verse is that obedience to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله is conditioned upon "what is right" (ma'ruf), even though the Prophet is infallible and never commands what is wrong. However, this expression may serve as a model for all commands issued by Islamic leaders. These commands are respected and enforceable only if they are consistent with the teachings of the Quran and the principles of Islamic Sharia, thus becoming an instance of "they will not disobey you in what is right" (لَا يَعْصِينَكَ فِي مَعْرُوفٍ).

How far are those who consider the commands of rulers as obligatory to obey, whatever they may be and from whomever they may come—something that is consistent neither with reason nor with the rulings of Sharia and the Quran.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in the famous letter he wrote to the people of Egypt regarding the governorship of Malik al-Ashtar, despite all the high descriptions he gave of Malik, concluded by saying:

"So listen to him and obey his command in whatever accords with the truth, for he is a sword among the swords of Allah" [2].

[1]: Refer to Volume 22 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 69 to 78 (under verse 18 of Surah Al-Fath). [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 276. Al-Qurtubi also cited this story in his commentary with slight differences, as did Al-Suyuti in Al-Durr al-Manthur and Abu al-Futuh in the commentary Rawh al-Jinan (under the verses discussed). Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ قَوْمًا غَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ قَدْ يَئِسُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ كَمَا يَئِسَ ٱلْكُفَّارُ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْقُبُورِ (١٣)
O you who have faith! Do not befriend a people at whom Allah is wrathful: they have despaired of the Hereafter, just as the faithless have despaired of the occupants of the graves. (13)

Commentary

Do Not Befriend This Wrath-Afflicted People

As we have seen, this Surah began with the issue of severing ties with the enemies of Allah and concludes with the same matter. In other words, the end of the Surah is a return to its beginning. It says: "O you who have believed, do not make allies of a people with whom Allah has become angry" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَوَلَّوْا قَوْمًا غَضِبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ).

You must not choose them as friends or place your secrets at their disposal.

Regarding who the "people with whom Allah has become angry" are, some explicitly consider it a reference to the Jews, as they are mentioned by this title in other verses of the Quran (as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 90 regarding the Jews: "So they have drawn on themselves wrath upon wrath" فَباؤُ بِغَضَبٍ عَلى‌ غَضَبٍ).

This interpretation is also consistent with the occasion of revelation (shan al-nuzul) mentioned for the verse, as it is said that a group of poor Muslims used to take news of the Muslims to the Jews, and in return, the Jews would give them gifts from the fruits of their trees; the above verse was revealed and forbade them [1]. However, despite this, the expression of the verse has a broad and extensive meaning that includes all disbelievers and polytheists. The expression of "wrath" in the Holy Quran is not exclusive to the "Jews," but has also been used regarding the hypocrites (Al-Fath, 6), and the occasion of revelation does not limit the general meaning of the verse.

Therefore, what is stated in this verse is consistent with the broad subject mentioned in the first verse of the Surah under the title of befriending the enemies of Allah.

Then, it mentions a point that serves as the reason for this prohibition, saying:

"They have despaired of [reward in] the Hereafter just as the disbelievers buried in graves have despaired" (قَدْ يَئِسُوا مِنَ الْآخِرَةِ كَمَا يَئِسَ الْكُفَّارُ مِنْ أَصْحَابِ الْقُبُورِ) [2]. This is because the dead among the disbelievers see the results of their actions in the world of Barzakh (the Purgatory) and have no way to return to make amends; thus, they are completely despaired. This group of the living is also so polluted with sin that they have no hope for their own salvation, exactly like the dead among the disbelievers.

Such individuals are certainly dangerous and untrustworthy; there is no reliance on their word, nor any credibility to their honesty or sincerity. They are despaired of the mercy of

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 276.

[^2]: Some have offered other possibilities in the interpretation of this verse, including: they are despaired of the reward of the Hereafter just as the polytheists are despaired of the resurrection of the inhabitants of the graves. However, the interpretation mentioned above is more appropriate (Note that according to the first interpretation, "مِنْ أَصْحَابِ الْقُبُورِ" is a description for the disbelievers, and according to the latter interpretation, it relates to "يئس" [despaired]).

the Truth (Allah), and for this reason, they commit any crime. Given this, how can you trust them and form bonds of friendship with them?!

O Allah! Never deprive us of Your endless grace nor make us despair of it. O Lord! Grant us such success that we may always be friends with Your friends and enemies to Your enemies, and remain steadfast in the path of Your friendship.

O Allah! Grant us success in following the example of Your noble Awliya (saints) and Prophets.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. 5th of Ramadan al-Mubarak, 1406 25/2/65 (Solar Hijri)
